sentences.text: Well, we-- we didn't realize, being children.
To us-- to us, the beginning of the war was very exciting.
We saw airplanes and tanks and soldiers marching.
The Germans, they came the Friday that was just a-- but Saturday morning, the army marched in.
The German Army marched in.
No, no, no, we couldn't have gone, in any case, because the air raids.
And people would be too frightened.
There was the shelling.
And-- but I think Friday afternoon it stopped.
The shelling stopped after this German delegation came.
And on Saturday morning, we saw the main German Army marching in.
And they had bands playing, and the German population came out.
And they were kissing and hugging each other, the soldiers with the German population.
And there was throwing flowers, like a conquering army normally when it enters a city.
And also our German neighbors, before the war broke out, she had-- I'm saying she because the mother-- the father wasn't alive anymore.
But the mother that had a grocery shop on the same block that we lived, and she had teenage children.
Before the war broke out, these children disappeared.
We didn't see them for months.
As soon as the Germans came, they reappeared, the teenagers, with nice black uniforms which had swastikas on their arm bands.
And the Germans, I must say when they came in, they looked-- they were-- the Polish troops were, when they ran away, they were without shoes, without uniforms, you know, like a defeated army.
And the Germans, they came in.
They were on nice couches.
They were sitting on coaches, and they looked nice, rested.
And they had tailored uniforms on, and they looked spick and span.
And to us, this was very exciting.
And they were throwing sweets to everybody.
They didn't-- there were placards put up just before they came in.
The population should not worry.
No harm will be done to the local population, and we should welcome the-- these-- the troops.
And this went on the whole day Saturday.
Then two soldiers knocked on our apartment door.
And they asked my father-- opposite us was a shop that sold guns, hunting guns and all kinds of guns, a big shop.
There were lovely shops where we lived down there.
So these soldiers asked who were the owners-- who were the owners of the shop, so my father told them who the owner is.
So they went over to the owner, and they brought him back.
And he had to open the shop.
And they drove up in a lorry.
They emptied the whole shop of all the guns, and they went away.
But on that Saturday, we still didn't feel anything bad.
But on-- on Sunday morning, opposite us was a hotel, and the German troops occupied that hotel.
And we could see-- and they parked their cars just outside the block where we lived.
And as we looked out of the window, they started-- and the Jews went walking by.
So they started getting hold of the Jews, and they made them clean the cars.
And they started calling them all different abusive names.
Then we realized we in trouble.
Well, the Poles just gave us abuse, but they didn't make us clean their cars.
And they didn't them lay on the floor and-- and scrub the-- the pavements.
That was already on the first Sunday they started doing that.
And this went on-- it increased as time went by, day by day.
They started doing worse things than this.
For instance, food became very scarce.
You had to queue up to get bread.
So if they-- so the German soldiers used to go by, or the police, German police.
They were local.
They rounded up local Germans, and they gave them uniforms.
And they became police.
The Polish police were still around as well at that time.
So they told the Polish police to take out the Jews from the queues.
We shouldn't be able to buy the bread.
So we had to buy the bread from-- from the Poles.
The Poles bought the bread, and they sold it to us Jews.
It made no difference.
Polish bread-- Jewish bread [INAUDIBLE] Polish bread, because we were only too glad to get bread because it was very scarce.
We-- we-- we already had to buy bread wherever we could, whether it was from Jewish or non-Jewish bakers.
But we-- it made no difference.
The Jewish bakers also had to bake the bread and sell it to the public.
They couldn't sell it to Jews.
You had to queue up to get the bread.
So--
Yes, then, we did, kept kosher, yes.
But the bread-- bread is not like meat, you know.
If you can't get kosher bread, you can buy non-kosher bread.
If there's no choice, you can buy non-kosher bread.
Anyway, I can't remember whether it was a kosher bakery or not a kosher bakery.
All I remember is then my mother and my father didn't dare to go out anymore to queue up for the bread.
So my mother bought us two-- for me and my brother, two caps like the Poles wore so we could stand in the queue and buy the bread.
Otherwise, you would have had to buy-- buy from the Poles.
And they charged a lot of-- a lot more than what we normally paid.
And this went on for a few days.
Then gradually they introduced other-- that we can't walk in the street after-- before 8:00 and after 5:00 in the afternoon Jews were forbidden to walk in the street.
And Jews were forbidden to travel by public transport.
And we were not allowed to walk in the-- we lived in the, as I told you before, at [?
Vikovska ?] Street, which was the main thoroughfare.
So we were not allowed to walk in that street.
Jews were not allowed to walk in that street.
So in order that we-- if we wanted to get out of the apartment, to walk-- not to walk in the street, but to get through the street and to the side streets, we had to buy a permit, a special permit.
For all the people that lived in the street, in that street, had to buy permits.
And they charged I think 5 or 10 marks per permit, which was a lot of money.
But you had to have it in order to buy-- to get out into the side streets.
And this went on far a few weeks like that.
And they-- every day, they brought in new laws.
Jews should have to wear a star of-- a yellow Star of David on the front and in the back.
Jewish businesses had to put out a notice that they're owned by Jews.
Jewish bakers were only allowed to bake bread, not cake or biscuits.
Jewish businesses, the big businesses, they sent in the commissars, they were called, officials, to see how the business is run.
And after a few weeks, once they got to know how the businesses is run, they told the owner to stay at home.
You shouldn't come in anymore.
And they took over the business.
Well, we weren't big enough for that, maybe just to the shop.
I'm talking now about big businesses.
Our-- our business, it closed down.
We couldn't open anymore just the same.
[PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] And then an order came out that Lodz is being annexed to the German Reich, which means that Lodz has to be Judenrein.
It has to be cleansed of Jews.
That's what it means.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my father decided-- the Germans, you see, they took a part of Poland and made it-- they annexed it to the German Reich.
The center of Poland they made a protectorate, a Polish protectorate.
And the eastern part of Poland was occupied by Russia, as you know.
So the Jews were-- they were-- we were told that the Jews can only go into the protectorate.
All the Jews from the part that is being annexed by Germany have to be clean of Jews.
And all the Jews can go to and settle in the protectorate.
So we had an uncle living in a little town which was-- it was called Opatow, a little town which was part of the protectorate.
So my father decided we should go and live there.
So he hired a car, a horse and car, and not just one horse, a few horses.
And he loaded all of the valuable things, clothes, books, whatever he possessed that was of value, couldn't-- couldn't load everything.
And we started on our journey to this town, Opatow.
On the way out from Lodz, we were stopped by two German policemen.
They were local Germans.
And we were ordered to, when we live the carriage, to proceed to a police station.
And as soon as my father saw what was happening, he ran away because he knew a man-- for a man, it was dangerous to be in that situation.
So my mother and the three children of us, we went along with these two German policemen to the police station.
They told us to unload everything from this carriage into this lorry.
We took everything inside, and they started opening up everything.
And they took out whatever they fancied for themselves.
And then one of them started looking through the-- my father had a lot of books.
A lot of them were Talmudic books.
So he started to open one of the-- of the Old Testament books.
And he started reading in Hebrew.
One of the officers there, he could read Hebrew.
Amazing, I was-- I was surprised.
And he started reading out.
He made a mockery out of it.
He said, your Moses, your prophet, Moses, he was a bastard.
And they found him-- and they-- they found him in the-- in the river.
And he, you know, he just went on for-- and we just couldn't-- couldn't say anything.
We just were sitting there, terrified.
In the morning, they gave us back whatever they didn't fancy.
And they loaded it on the carriage, and they let us go.
And we travelled like this for two days.
It was in-- in the winter.
It was the end of 1939.
Then we came to the-- we reached this place, Opatow.
And we stayed there, and then my father joined us.
He somehow managed to get to us.
And we stayed in this place for about six weeks.
But it was impossible to live there.
You see, thousands and thousands of people came from different-- from the territory that was occupied by the-- was-- was annexed into Germany.
So being a little town, we-- there was not enough to eat.
And there was no room there to sleep.
My-- my uncle didn't have room for us.
And then we had, you see-- and the Germans, I didn't know that then.
But from what I've read, what went on, the Germans wanted to get rid of the Jews from the Reich to start with.
But there was the governor of the protectorate, his name, I've read it in a book somewhere.
His name was Frank.
You might have heard of him.
And he-- maybe a lot of Poles also were driven out, not just Jews.
So there were a few million people in that area.
And he couldn't cope with all these people.
He was frightened there would be a riot.
There would be an uprising.
So he protested the whole time to Germany, to Berlin, that this can't go on.
So they had to look for different ways what to do with the Jews.
So then we had-- but we didn't know all this was going on.
I only read this after the war.
But at the time, we heard that Lodz was quieting down, and they're not driving out any more Jews.
So my father-- and we had-- there we had nothing to eat in this [INAUDIBLE] we-- we-- no-- no more food.
Everything was very expensive.
We used up practically all the money we had to buy food.
And at home, before the war broke out, my father bought-- we had a basement full of everything-- sacks of sugar, sacks of flour, of rice, of-- of-- loads of potatoes, coal.
He bought everything or whatever he could because he knew the money won't be worth much.
So he exchanged everything into goods.
Cloth, he bought loads of cloth.
He had-- wherever he could find a place where to hide it, the--
So he said, let's go back home.
We had-- we've got all this at home.
Why starve here?
Why pay all this money?
We had no more money, in any case.
So we decided-- he hired-- we couldn't go by train or by-- by-- so we hired a Pole with a carriage, with a little lorry and a horse.
And we went back to Lodz.
We came back to Lodz, and for a few weeks it was quiet.
We didn't-- there was the usual-- now and then we had-- they got hold of some Jews.
And they took them into a place, and they shot them.
And some of them were sent away, this and that.
But it was nothing more than that was what they did in the protectorate.
So we decided to stay in Lodz.
There was one [LAUGHS] story.
I must mention it.
A neighbor of ours, her husband-- that was before we went out.
He ran out into the street, and he wanted to buy some bread.
And he never came back again, so his wife was worried what happened to him.
The next day, he turned up, this husband.
So he told her a story.
They took him into a place with about 20 or 30 Jews in this place.
And-- and they gave him a good beating.
And then a man came in with a white overall.
And they took one man out.
And they-- and they heard screaming.
This man was screaming.
They were beating him up.
And this man with the white overall came back.
He had a knife in his hand, full of blood.
And his overall was full of blood.
So anybody-- and they did that one by one.
They took these people out, and you heard them scream.
And the people that were left all the time thought that they were being killed with that knife.
Anyway, in the meantime they didn't do-- they only beat up these people.
But later on, they let them all go, just to frighten them.
They did it for a-- for a joke, to make the people frightened.
They should think that they're being killed with a knife.
They used to get a hold of a man with a beard, cut him off a half a bread.
He should look silly, you know.
They left him the half a beard, so he had to walk around with his face covered.
He didn't want to cut off the other half a beard.
If he was very orthodox, he wouldn't want to cut it off.
So he walked around-- he didn't want to walk around with half a beard, so he covered his face with a scarf.
And things like that they did, but there was no mass killing going on in the beginning.
Until-- this went on until the beginning-- yes, and then they-- we heard that there is going to be-- they're not going to expel all the Jews from Lodz.
But they're going to make a ghetto.
They will form a ghetto.
And they-- out in the paper, there was a plan where the ghetto was going to be.
And every day, a different street was going to be moved into the ghetto.
And the people that lived in the ghetto, the Germans and the Jews, were going to be moved out from the ghetto.
And this-- according to this plan, they were going to take maybe 18 months or 2 years to transfer a quarter of a million Jews into the ghetto.
And to move out all the other people out of the ghetto, to give them-- to settle them in, it was a long, drawn-out process.
So we, you know, being optimists, we thought, oh, the war will be finished by then.
Till they get around to do all these things, it will be all over.
So one afternoon, my mother went to the market.
It was on a Thursday afternoon.
I remembered like it will be now, although it was 55 years ago, 56 years ago.
1940, it was in March.
It's-- it's near 57 years.
My mother went to the market to buy chicken, meat.
You could still get what you wanted if you had the money.
And it was scarce, but-- but you could still buy.
As she was coming home, she saw a lot of soldiers in our area, German soldiers.
She didn't take much notice of it.
She came in, and she told my father this, that there were more soldiers than usual.
And as I looked out the window, [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [DRINKS] I saw through the window soldiers surrounding the block where we lived.
And I told this to my father.
And he didn't dare go to the window in case he was seen.
But we children, we took a chance.
We looked out.
And as I told my father what's going on outside, so he said, put on-- it was in the winter, March.
It was still very cold.
So he told us to put on our coats and our rucksacks.
And as he was talking to us, we heard shots coming from the yard, from the staircase, and a bang on the door.
Two soldiers came in.
And they said, we'll give you 15 minutes time to be outside in the yard.
So we were already in our coats, and we started walking into the yard.
We got into the staircase.
And as we came into the staircase, they got hold of my father.
They dragged him back into the apartment.
And us they pushed out into the yard.
And we had shots all the time.
And we couldn't believe that they're shooting people.
We thought they just wanted to frighten us to get us out into the yard quick.
And as I came into the yard, there was snow all over the place.
And I saw a woman laying in a pool of blood.
I couldn't believe it.
And they pushed us into the gate.
On the continent, they have got such gates.
In the building is a gate.
So the women and the children, they pushed us into the gate.
And the soldiers went back into the apartment, and we heard shots all the time for about a half an hour.
And then they came down.
And I noticed they had the swastikas, the scout-- what's it called?
The skeleton, the skulls, you know, on their helmets, and we realized that they are the SS.
And they pushed us out into the street.
It was already getting dark.
It was about 5 o'clock in the afternoon.
As we came into the street, the streets were deserted of people.
There was nobody about except every few yards there was a soldier with a machine gun.
And they escorted us all the way.
And all the way-- and on the way, they knocked everything down.
Whatever we managed to carry with us, they knocked it out of our hands.
And this went on till we finally reached the part where the ghetto was going to be.
When we reached the ghetto-- it wasn't cordoned off of anything.
It was just we knew the streets, according to the plan that we saw in the paper, that there was going to be the ghetto.
For the first time, I saw Jewish police.
They didn't have uniforms.
They just had an armband with a Star of David on it.
And we were told, this is the Jewish police.
And they handed us over to the Jewish-- the Gestapo, the SS handed us over to the Jewish police.
And we were taken to a school.
And we were put into the school there.
And we stayed in the school for about two hours in the middle of the night.
It was already late.
And they announced-- the Jewish police announced that whoever has relations living in the ghetto can go to join up with the relations.
And whoever hasn't should stay in the school till they allotted some place where to live.
So we had an uncle living not far from there, so we went along there.
And in the middle of the night-- they were all asleep-- we banged on the door.
They opened the door.
We came in.
We told them what happened, and we all started crying.
And in the morning, my mother and my sister went along to the headquarters of the Jewish administration to find out what happened to my father.
So when they came along there, there were already a lot of women there.
And this-- for the first time, my mother met this-- his name was Chaim Rumkowski.
And he was the-- appointed by the Germans to be the leader of the Jews in Lodz.
By that time, it wasn't called Lodz anymore.
It was called Litzmannstadt.
They changed the name.
When they annexed the city to the Reich, they changed all the names of the streets and of the town.
They called it Litzmannstadt.
So they gave him the title, the eldest of the Juden of Litzmannstatd, the oldest of-- the eldest-- the eldest of the Jews of the Litzmannstadt ghetto.
So--
